quotes.verbatim_quote:
It is most common to find mixed harmonic movement. This example starts on a | chord and
moves to a V chord which sets it up for a cycle move to Cmaj7 in the second measure. Notice that
the lowest note of Gmaj7 in the first measure is D, the fifth, and the lowest note of G7(5) is b5.
This was done to continue the effect of chromatic movement from Gmaj7 to G7(5) to Cmaj7. The
harmony then moves by half step to the finish.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Voice-leading through bass-note choice for chromatic continuity.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In many cases the diminished seventh chord is the in-between, the hamburger helper of ascend-
ing harmonic movement.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Diminished seventh as standard connector chord for ascending sequences.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The bass notes of the first two measures are the same as the chord names above.

These measures have doubled bass notes, a style used by musicians playing upright bass in the
swing era of jazz. Otherwise, the movement of harmony is the same as the first example above.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Doubled-bass stylistic device anchored in swing-era practice.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In this self accompanying style the bass alternates between the root and the fifth of each chord.
Root/fifth is often associated with simple music, such as polka bands, country music and the like.
Root/fifth is used in jazz too, but usually not in such a rigid oom-pah fashion.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines root/fifth alternation with stylistic warning.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Keeping a strict pattern of root/seventh is not as important as long as the effect
(low to high alternation) is there.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Effect matters more than strict interval adherence.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
One of the most common bass note moves is approaching notes — especially

indicated. In bar one the chromatic passing
quotes.why_load_bearing: Chromatic approach-note rule defining jazz walking bass.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Leading with a bass note is a way to give variety to accompaniment instead of just chugging
along with four bass notes to a measure.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Anticipation as device to break four-on-the-floor monotony.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The opposite of leading with the lower line is trailing or delaying it. This too steers away from
heaviness.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Delay as paired counterpart to anticipation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
For slower moving tunes, arpeggiation is a natural for fingerstyle playing. Don't forget to empha-
size the melody note so that it won't get lost in the sea of eighth note soup.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Arpeggiation appropriate for slow tunes, melody projection required.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A melody accompanied by two additional notes is often all you need to make an arrangement
sound full, whether you are playing by yourself or with a group.
You can pluck the two inner voices with i and m, or strike them both with p.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Right-hand fingering options for two-note inner-voice work.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Often the melody may merge with and become the top part of the two note bass accompaniment.

It's not a problem, it’s a solution.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Reframes voice-leading collision as valid arrangement choice.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Moving tenths in the left hand was one of the primary accompanying devices of pianists in the
swing era. Fats Waller, Willie “The Lion” Smith, Art Tatum, Teddy Wilson, and many others made
this the style until bebop came along. Within reason, you can emulate it on the guitar. The pianists
mentioned often kept almost constant quarter-note moving tenths going with the melody. You
should be able to manage at least half notes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines moving-tenths device with historical source and adaptation target.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This variation uses the same chords except the accompaniment is voiced so that the harmony
moves in alternating pairs of fourths and tritones. The bottom note in each pair alternates between
major or minor seventh to major third. There is no explicitly stated root or fifth of the chord. If you play
in a group, the bassist will cover roots and fifths.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Rootless close-voicing system using fourths and tritones.
